title: Novo Nordisk’s weight-loss drug patent loss sparks a ‘gold rush’ for generic drugmakers
summary: India's weight-loss drug market is seeing a major shift. Generic versions of Novo Nordisk's semaglutide are now available at lower prices. This is impacting Eli Lilly's drug sales. The market is expected to grow significantly. Indian drugmakers are launching...
thbAction: Position THB's pharma CRM and omnichannel HCP engagement workflows to support launch planning, KAM orchestration, and compliant follow-up across India and the Middle East.
contentHtml:
A surge of low-cost copies of Novo Nordisk’s blockbuster drug semaglutide in India is rapidly reshaping the country’s fast-growing obesity market. The influx is eating into Eli Lilly & Co.’s early lead and providing an early test of how Novo could perform as its patents expire globally, reported Bloomberg.

Within days of dozens of generics entering the Indian market, semaglutide’s share in the GLP-1 segment rose to 33% in March, up from 25% a month earlier, Bloomberg reported citing researcher Pharmarack. The gain came at the cost of tirzepatide, the active ingredient in Lilly’s Mounjaro, whose share declined to 64% from 71%, according to Pharmarack.

Also Read: A blockbuster unleashed- What happens when a big fat business explodes?

The data, which tracks sales of non-branded versions between March 21 and 31, provides the first meaningful snapshot of demand after India opened its market to off-patent semaglutide. While Canada was the first country to lose patent protection for the drug in January, regulators there have yet to approve any generic versions.

It also offers an early indication of the global wave of generic competition that Eli Lilly is likely to face as semaglutide nears patent cliffs in other markets. The emerging price war and spike in demand are being closely monitored, with India serving as a key test case for how rapidly generics can chip away at a branded drug’s dominance as patents expire across major emerging economies.

Mounjaro sales in March dropped to Rs 1.14 billion rupees in March from Rs 1.35 billion rupees a month earlier, Pharmarack data show. Lilly will lose exclusivity on the drug next decade.

Also Read: Rs 450 to Rs 4,200- Drugmakers rush in to win the business of losing weight

As semaglutide lost its patent protection, weight-loss drugs based on this compound are expected to become highly popular due to their affordable, generic pricing. As per market research firm PharmaTrack, the semaglutide market is poised for massive expansion with the obesity drug segment projected to grow to ₹8,000 crore by 2030 from approximately ₹1,500 crore.

Leading drugmakers including Sun Pharmaceuticals, Dr Reddy’s Laboratories, Zydus Lifesciences, Natco Pharma, Eris Lifesciences, USV, Glenmark, Torrent and Alkem Laboratorieswere among the first to roll out generic semaglutide on the first day of patent expiry of Novo Nordisk’s innovator molecule. These companies are offering the generic version at significantly affordable prices, seeking to capture the lucrative post-patent market opportunity in the domestic market as well as overseas.

'Gold rush phase'

This is a “gold rush phase” as generics come on board, Sheetal Sapale, vice president of commercial at Pharmarack told Bloomberg. Though Mounjaro still dominates the market in India, it’s taken a “major hit” following the launch of semaglutide copies, she said.

Torrent Pharmaceuticals Ltd. has emerged as the biggest local player in semaglutide after Novo, trumping rivals including Sun Pharmaceutical Industries Ltd. and Dr Reddy’s Laboratories Ltd. Torrent has an 8% market share, while Novo still holds 70%, according to Pharmarack.

Indian drugmakers moved quickly after semaglutide’s patent expired on March 20, rolling out versions that cost as little as 1,290 rupees. Volumes of semaglutide drugs more than doubled in March compared to the month before, Pharmarack said.

More aggressive launches are expected in the coming days, Sapale said, likely driving exponential growth in India’s weight-loss drug market.

Why are weight loss pills gaining traction?

Price won’t be the sole driver of sales of popular weight-loss and diabetes drug, semaglutide, in India. Robust cold chains, device quality, and long-term patient support are also critical pillars, reported quoting the doctors.

As per the ET report, executives said while aggressive pricing—as low as below Rs 1,300 for a month's treatment or more than 85% discount to innovator price—is key to enhancing affordability and access beyond the metros, companies are also looking to differentiate themselves through device innovation and patient support ecosystems.

From pre-filled disposable pens and reusable multi-dose devices to vial formats, drugmakers are tailoring delivery mechanisms to improve ease of use, dosing flexibility, and patient comfort.

Drugmakers are also investing in education and support infrastructure such as metabolic care centres, nutritional guidance, and onboarding programmes to help patients navigate any side effects and build confidence.
